summary: Scout structurally-ungreppable A/B questions across 5 indexed repos: build enumerable ground truth, assess judgeability + divergence likelihood

result.question: What is the complete set of non-test functions/methods that are transitively affected by a change to `get_srid_info()` in `django/contrib/gis/db/models/fields.py`? List every function that directly or indirectly calls this function (across all call depths).
result.groundTruth:
Depth 1 — direct callers in django/contrib/gis/db/models/fields.py:
1. BaseSpatialField::spheroid — django/contrib/gis/db/models/fields.py:122
2. BaseSpatialField::units — django/contrib/gis/db/models/fields.py:125
3. BaseSpatialField::units_name — django/contrib/gis/db/models/fields.py:128
4. BaseSpatialField::geodetic — django/contrib/gis/db/models/fields.py:131

Depth 2 — callers of spheroid/units/units_name/geodetic:
5. BaseSpatialOperations::get_area_att_for_field — django/contrib/gis/db/backends/base/operations.py:194
6. BaseSpatialOperations::get_distance_att_for_field — django/contrib/gis/db/backends/base/operations.py:206
7. MySQLOperations::get_distance — django/contrib/gis/db/backends/mysql/operations.py:120
8. OracleOperations::get_distance — django/contrib/gis/db/backends/oracle/operations.py:180
9. PostGISOperations::get_distance — django/contrib/gis/db/backends/postgis/operations.py:270
10. SpatiaLiteOperations::get_distance — django/contrib/gis/db/backends/spatialite/operations.py:129
11. Area::as_sql — django/contrib/gis/db/models/functions.py:166
12. Area::as_sqlite — django/contrib/gis/db/models/functions.py:175
13. Distance::as_postgresql — django/contrib/gis/db/models/functions.py:322
14. Distance::as_sqlite — django/contrib/gis/db/models/functions.py:353
15. Length::as_sql — django/contrib/gis/db/models/functions.py:474
16. Length::as_postgresql — django/contrib/gis/db/models/functions.py:484
17. Length::as_sqlite — django/contrib/gis/db/models/functions.py:502
18. Perimeter::as_postgresql — django/contrib/gis/db/models/functions.py:544
19. Perimeter::as_sqlite — django/contrib/gis/db/models/functions.py:555

Depth 3 — callers of get_area_att_for_field and get_distance_att_for_field:
20. AreaField::get_db_prep_value — django/contrib/gis/db/models/sql/conversion.py:24
21. AreaField::from_db_value — django/contrib/gis/db/models/sql/conversion.py:30
22. DistanceField::get_db_prep_value — django/contrib/gis/db/models/sql/conversion.py:57
23. DistanceField::from_db_value — django/contrib/gis/db/models/sql/conversion.py:67

Depth 3 — callers of concrete get_distance() implementations (via dynamic dispatch through connection.ops.get_distance()):
24. DistanceLookupBase::process_distance — django/contrib/gis/db/models/lookups.py:319

Depth 4 — callers of DistanceLookupBase::process_distance:
25. DWithinLookup::process_distance — django/contrib/gis/db/models/lookups.py:338
26. DWithinLookup::process_rhs — django/contrib/gis/db/models/lookups.py:351
27. DistanceLookupFromFunction::as_sql — django/contrib/gis/db/models/lookups.py:359

Note: Items 24-27 (lookups.py chain) are reached via dynamic dispatch (connection.ops.get_distance() → concrete backend impls). Items 20-23 (conversion.py) are statically reachable and are the key discriminating nodes between OmniWeave and grep-only agents. Total: 27 production-code nodes across 4 hops, spanning 7 files in 5 directories.
result.omniweaveAnswer: omniweave impact get_srid_info -d 4 returns 28 nodes. Correctly finds all 19 depth-1+depth-2 nodes (fields.py×4, base/operations.py×2, mysql×1, oracle×1, postgis×1, spatialite×1, functions.py×9) and all 4 conversion.py depth-3 nodes (items 20-23). However includes 4 FALSE POSITIVES via super() call misresolution: IsEmpty::as_sqlite (functions.py:454), AsGeoJSON::as_oracle (functions.py:205), AsGML::as_oracle (functions.py:222), FromWKB::as_oracle (functions.py:384) — these call super().as_sql() not geodetic/spheroid, verified by source reading. OmniWeave MISSES the lookups.py chain (items 24-27) because it resolves connection.ops.get_distance() to abstract BaseSpatialOperations::get_distance which raises NotImplementedError, failing to follow the dynamic dispatch to concrete backends. At default depth=2, OmniWeave returns only depth-1+depth-2 nodes (19 correct) and misses all 8 depth-3+ nodes.
result.grepCanReach: partial — grep can structurally follow each hop individually (grep for get_srid_info → 4 callers; grep for geodetic/spheroid/units_name → 15 callers; grep for get_area_att_for_field → 4 conversion.py callers), BUT requires deliberate 3-level recursive expansion across 12 files in 6 directories. The without-arm agent plausibly truncates at depth-2 (19 nodes) after finding the large fan-out of 15 depth-2 callers, and specifically misses the depth-3 conversion.py nodes because it must know to re-grep using the abstract backend method names get_area_att_for_field and get_distance_att_for_field as new search terms. The lookups.py chain (items 24-27) is structurally blocked for grep because the call goes through connection.ops.get_distance() — dynamic dispatch that grep cannot resolve without reading all 4 concrete backend files and matching them to the lookup call site.
result.divergencePrediction: The without-arm agent using grep will plausibly find 19/27 nodes (the depth-1 and depth-2 callers). It will MISS: (a) the 4 depth-3 conversion.py nodes (AreaField::get_db_prep_value, AreaField::from_db_value, DistanceField::get_db_prep_value, DistanceField::from_db_value) because after collecting 15 depth-2 callers the agent rarely performs a 3rd round of grepping on the intermediate abstract method names; (b) the 4 lookups.py depth-3/4 nodes because they require resolving dynamic dispatch through connection.ops. The structural reason for divergence: multi-hop transitive closure with 15 intermediate nodes creates combinatorial search space that grep-only agents pruned by heuristic depth-2 limits will miss. OmniWeave at depth=3+ automatically traverses the conversion.py chain, giving it a clear 4-node advantage on the cleanest missing subset.
result.notes: OmniWeave at depth=3 gets 23/27 correct (19 depth-2 + 4 conversion.py), with 4 false positives from super() call misresolution in GIS functions. The clearest A/B discriminator is the 4 conversion.py depth-3 nodes (items 20-23): OmniWeave finds them, grep-only agents plausibly miss them. The lookups.py chain (items 24-27) requires dynamic dispatch resolution and is missed by BOTH OmniWeave and grep — making it a harder theoretical question but not useful for A/B discrimination. For evaluation purposes, scoring could require items 1-23 (statically reachable) as the core set with items 24-27 as bonus. The false positive rate for OmniWeave (4/28 = 14%) is a notable caveat for the WITH-arm quality, but does not change the recommendation since the WITHOUT-arm agent will have MORE misses.

result.question: When the user runs `quartet.py CentroMiner` (alias `cm`), which script handles it, and what external command-line tools does that script invoke as subprocesses (including those inside its plotting branch)?
result.groundTruth:
Hop 1 — quartet.py dispatches to: quartet_centrominer.py (quartet.py:35, via subprocess.run(['python3', f'{sys.path[0]}/quartet_centrominer.py'] + parameter))

Hop 2 — quartet_centrominer.py invokes these external CLI tools:
1. trf — quartet_centrominer.py:40 (via quartet_util.runsub, tandem repeat finder)
2. cd-hit-est — quartet_centrominer.py:61 (via quartet_util.runsub, sequence clustering)
3. makeblastdb — quartet_centrominer.py:63 (via quartet_util.runsub, build BLAST database)
4. blastn — quartet_centrominer.py:65 (via quartet_util.runsub, nucleotide BLAST)
5. Rscript — quartet_centrominer.py:187 (via subprocess.run directly, for per-chromosome ggplot2 plotting; only when --noplot is not set)

Total: 5 external tools. All 4 of trf/cd-hit-est/makeblastdb/blastn also appear in check_prerequisite at quartet_centrominer.py:277. Rscript does NOT appear in the prerequisite check — it is only invoked inside the `if noplot != True:` conditional block at line 171–187.
result.omniweaveAnswer: omniweave callees quartet.py → surfaces 4 file edges to the sibling scripts (quartet_assemblymapper.py, quartet_gapfiller.py, quartet_centrominer.py, quartet_teloexplorer.py) plus subprocess/sys imports. Correctly identifies the 4 sibling scripts. omniweave callees quartet_centrominer.py → lists: check_prerequisite (quartet_util.py:22), run (quartet_util.py:10), subprocess/os/sys/multiprocessing.dummy/argparse/itertools imports, file edge to quartet_util.py, and function centroMiner. DOES NOT surface trf, cd-hit-est, makeblastdb, blastn, or Rscript — the external subprocess tool calls are invisible at this level. omniweave callees quartet_util.py → only shows stdlib imports (time/sys/math/subprocess/os/re/collections); no external tool names surfaced.
result.grepCanReach: partial — Grep CAN reach the tool names for trf/cd-hit-est/makeblastdb/blastn because: (a) the string 'quartet_centrominer.py' appears literally as a substring in the f-string at quartet.py:35, so hop 1 is textually findable; (b) all 4 prerequisite-checked tools appear literally in the check_prerequisite call at centrominer:277 as a flat list ['trf','cd-hit-est','makeblastdb','blastn']. However, grep STRUCTURALLY MISSES Rscript: it is not in the prerequisite check, only in a deeply-nested conditional block at line 187 (inside centroblaster closure, inside `if noplot != True:` branch). A without-arm agent grepping for the tool list from prerequisite checks will enumerate only 4/5 tools and miss Rscript. The f-string interpolation `{sys.path[0]}/quartet_centrominer.py` is non-literal for the directory but the script basename is still textually present, so hop 1 does not constitute a hard structural failure for grep — just mild fragility.
result.divergencePrediction: WITHOUT-arm agent will plausibly answer with 4 tools (trf, cd-hit-est, makeblastdb, blastn) and MISS Rscript. Structural reason: agents short-circuit at the check_prerequisite() call at line 277 which is a clean explicit list — they read that list and stop, never reading 150+ lines deeper into the centroblaster closure to find the `subprocess.run(f"echo '{rscript}' | Rscript -", ...)` at line 187. Rscript is also absent from the module docstring/README prerequisites section. This is a plausible miss rate of roughly 60-80% for a without-arm agent that satisfices at the prerequisite list. A very thorough agent that reads the entire function body will find it. OmniWeave's callees cannot surface it either (it does not index subprocess string arguments as external tool nodes), so OmniWeave + agent reasoning both require the same file read — but the key is whether the agent STOPS at the prerequisite list or keeps reading.
result.notes: Ground truth size = 1 dispatched script + 5 external tools. The question has a cleanly enumerable answer (1+5 items). The best framing focuses on completeness: a partial answer listing only 4 tools (missing Rscript) is judged as INCOMPLETE. The f-string dispatch `{sys.path[0]}/quartet_centrominer.py` introduces mild structural fragility at hop 1 but the basename is textually present. The strongest divergence opportunity is Rscript at line 187 which is only reachable by reading the full centroblaster function body, not the prerequisite list. OmniWeave callees does not surface external tool strings at all — it only shows Python-level callees. So this question requires both the file-open operation AND careful full-function reading, making it a realistic partial-miss scenario for without-arm agents under token pressure.

result.question: The `plotMA` generic in DESeq2 is implemented by S4 methods. List every S4 method registered for `plotMA` in this package, the class each one dispatches on, and the name of the implementation function it delegates to.
result.groundTruth:
2 S4 methods registered for `plotMA` in DESeq2:
1. R/plots.R:232 — `setMethod("plotMA", signature(object="DESeqDataSet"), plotMA.DESeqDataSet)` — dispatches on class **DESeqDataSet**, delegates to implementation function `plotMA.DESeqDataSet` (defined at R/plots.R:111)
2. R/plots.R:237 — `setMethod("plotMA", signature(object="DESeqResults"), plotMA.DESeqResults)` — dispatches on class **DESeqResults**, delegates to implementation function `plotMA.DESeqResults` (defined at R/plots.R:120)

Note: `plotMA` is NOT defined via `setGeneric` in this package — the generic lives in BiocGenerics (imported via `import(BiocGenerics)` in NAMESPACE). DESeq2 only registers methods.

OmniWeave stores these as nodes `DESeqDataSet::plotMA` (method, R/plots.R:232) and `DESeqResults::plotMA` (method, R/plots.R:237) with `overrides` edges to the generic.

Grep cross-check: `grep -rn 'setMethod.*"plotMA"' R/` finds exactly these 2 lines, class names appear inline in `signature(object="CLASS")` on the same line — fully verifiable from source text.
result.omniweaveAnswer: omniweave node plotMA returns 2 method nodes: DESeqDataSet::plotMA at R/plots.R:232 and DESeqResults::plotMA at R/plots.R:237. The DB confirms both nodes with qualified_name encoding the dispatch class. omniweave query plotMA surfaces both method nodes plus plotMA.DESeqDataSet and plotMA.DESeqResults as separate function nodes. The 'overrides' edges (kind='overrides', metadata synthesizedBy='r-s4-dispatch') connect each method node back to the generic. callers of plotMA.DESeqResults shows 1 caller: plotMA.DESeqDataSet (a direct function call at line 117, not via S4 dispatch).
result.grepCanReach: yes — `grep -rn 'setMethod.*"plotMA"'` on the R/ directory returns exactly 2 lines (plots.R:232 and plots.R:237). Both class names appear on the same line in `signature(object="CLASS")`. The implementation function names (3rd argument) are also on those same lines. All three pieces of information (generic name, dispatch class, implementation function) are co-located in the textual source. An agent reading those 2 lines has the full answer without needing to follow any non-textual edge.
result.divergencePrediction: Without-arm agent will likely get this CORRECT if it knows to grep for setMethod. The plausible failure mode is (a) missing that the `plotMA` generic is from BiocGenerics (no setGeneric in this package) which may confuse an agent looking for the generic definition, or (b) confusing the 3 implementation functions (plotMA.DESeqDataSet, plotMA.DESeqResults, plotMA.dataframe) with the 2 registered S4 methods — the agent might list 3 or conflate them. However, a careful grep+read agent that looks specifically for `setMethod("plotMA", ...)` lines will find both methods correctly. The structural reason divergence is only "plausible" not "certain": the class attribution lives on the SAME LINE as setMethod, so no multi-file traversal is needed. This limits the structural advantage of OmniWeave's pre-indexed dispatch graph for this specific question.
result.notes: The question is well-specified and the ground truth is authoritative (2 methods, hand-verified by grep cross-check and DB inspection). However, I recommend=false because grep structurally CAN reach the full correct answer — both dispatch classes and implementation function names are explicit literal strings on the setMethod call lines. The only realistic failure is agent confusion between S4 method nodes vs plain implementation functions (plotMA.DESeqDataSet / plotMA.DESeqResults / plotMA.dataframe), or missing the BiocGenerics provenance of the generic. For a track-1 benchmark that requires grep to STRUCTURALLY fail, this is not the best candidate. A better candidate for this category would require cross-package inheritance tracing (e.g., what methods does DESeqDataSet inherit from RangedSummarizedExperiment) where the evidence lives outside this corpus entirely — but OmniWeave also cannot answer that. The plotMA question is a fair 'efficiency' question (OmniWeave returns the answer in 1 call vs grep+read in ~3 steps) but not a 'correctness' differentiator.

result.question: In `src/vs/editor/contrib/suggest/browser/suggest.ts` at line 286, the call `_snippetSuggestSupport.provideCompletionItems(model, position, context, token)` is made inside the `provideSuggestionItems` function. The variable `_snippetSuggestSupport` is a module-level singleton declared at line 182 as `let _snippetSuggestSupport: languages.CompletionItemProvider | undefined`. In a production VS Code session, which concrete class's `provideCompletionItems` implementation executes at that call site, and in which file and line is that implementation defined?
result.groundTruth:
The concrete implementation that runs is `SnippetCompletionProvider.provideCompletionItems`, defined at `src/vs/workbench/contrib/snippets/browser/snippetCompletionProvider.ts:91`.

The binding is established at `src/vs/workbench/contrib/snippets/browser/snippetsService.ts:235` where `setSnippetSuggestSupport(new SnippetCompletionProvider(...))` is called — the only non-test call to `setSnippetSuggestSupport` in the entire codebase.

Cross-check via grep:
- `grep -rn "setSnippetSuggestSupport(" src/vs --include="*.ts"` returns exactly one non-test hit: `snippetsService.ts:235`
- `snippetsService.ts:235`: `setSnippetSuggestSupport(new SnippetCompletionProvider(this._languageService, this, languageConfigurationService));`
- `snippetCompletionProvider.ts:79`: `export class SnippetCompletionProvider implements CompletionItemProvider`
- `snippetCompletionProvider.ts:91`: `async provideCompletionItems(model: ITextModel, position: Position, context: CompletionContext): Promise<CompletionList>`

OmniWeave cross-check:
- `omniweave callers "SnippetCompletionProvider.provideCompletionItems"` → returns exactly 1 caller: `provideSuggestionItems` at `suggest.ts:214` ✓
- `omniweave callees "provideSuggestionItems"` → lists `SnippetCompletionProvider.provideCompletionItems` at `snippetCompletionProvider.ts:91` as a direct callee ✓
- `omniweave impact "SnippetCompletionProvider"` → shows `provideSuggestionItems` at `suggest.ts:214` as an affected symbol ✓

Ground truth items: 1 concrete implementation (`SnippetCompletionProvider.provideCompletionItems` at `snippetCompletionProvider.ts:91`), binding at `snippetsService.ts:235`.
result.grepCanReach: partial — grep can structurally reach the answer but requires a 5-hop chain across two module boundaries: (1) read `suggest.ts:182` to find the variable type `languages.CompletionItemProvider`; (2) find that the variable is only mutated by `setSnippetSuggestSupport(support)` at line 190; (3) grep for `setSnippetSuggestSupport(` across all files to find the one non-test call at `snippetsService.ts:235`; (4) read that line to see `new SnippetCompletionProvider(...)` is passed; (5) read `snippetCompletionProvider.ts:91` for the implementation. The binding is through a global-singleton setter pattern (`_snippetSuggestSupport` ← `setSnippetSuggestSupport(...)` in a different module), which is textually traceable but requires knowing to search for the setter call across the entire codebase. grep cannot "see" that there are 26 other `provideCompletionItems` implementations and pick the right one without following this chain.
result.divergencePrediction: Without OmniWeave, a coding agent is likely to get this INCOMPLETE or WRONG for two structural reasons: (1) Seeing `_snippetSuggestSupport: languages.CompletionItemProvider | undefined`, the agent knows it is SOME CompletionItemProvider but has 26+ definitions to choose from; a less thorough agent will list multiple candidates or say "any registered provider" rather than pinpointing `SnippetCompletionProvider`. (2) The singleton binding (`_snippetSuggestSupport = support` at suggest.ts:190, set from `snippetsService.ts:235`) lives in a DIFFERENT module behind an indirection — the agent must know to search for `setSnippetSuggestSupport(` across the whole codebase, find that only one non-test call exists, and read what concrete type it passes. If the agent stops at the interface declaration or lists all `provideCompletionItems` implementations without following the setter chain, it will produce an ambiguous or wrong answer. With OmniWeave, a single `callees "provideSuggestionItems"` tool call directly surfaces `SnippetCompletionProvider.provideCompletionItems` as the resolved callee.
result.omniweaveAnswer: omniweave callers "SnippetCompletionProvider.provideCompletionItems" → 1 result: `provideSuggestionItems` at `suggest.ts:214`. omniweave callees "provideSuggestionItems" → lists `SnippetCompletionProvider.provideCompletionItems` at `snippetCompletionProvider.ts:91` among 20 callees. omniweave impact "SnippetCompletionProvider" → shows `provideSuggestionItems` at `suggest.ts:214` as an affected symbol. OmniWeave resolves the dispatch edge correctly; the answer is directly accessible via a single `callees` query on the enclosing function.
result.notes: This is a TypeScript global-singleton setter dispatch pattern: a module-level variable `_snippetSuggestSupport` typed as an interface (`languages.CompletionItemProvider`) is set by a public setter function (`setSnippetSuggestSupport`) that is called from a different module. Grep returns 26+ `provideCompletionItems` definitions; the correct one is uniquely identified only by tracing the setter across module boundaries. OmniWeave resolves the call edge directly. The answer is cleanly enumerable (exactly 1 concrete implementation runs). The question is fair because a real coding agent would plausibly ask it when trying to understand the snippet completion code path. The without-arm agent can reach the answer but is likely to undershoot (list candidates) or miss the cross-module setter binding. Note: the emmet extension also appears in `provideSuggestionItems` callees via OmniWeave but uses a different interface signature (`vscode.CompletionContext` not `languages.CompletionContext`) and is registered via a different mechanism — not via `setSnippetSuggestSupport`. The snippet provider is the sole binding for the `_snippetSuggestSupport` global.

result.question: In `ImmutableSortedMap.reverseOrder()` (guava/src/com/google/common/collect/ImmutableSortedMap.java:618), the implementation is `return new Builder<>(Ordering.<K>natural().reverse())`. Which concrete `Ordering` subclass (not abstract) does the expression `Ordering.<K>natural().reverse()` actually return at runtime?
result.groundTruth:
ReverseNaturalOrdering (guava/src/com/google/common/collect/ReverseNaturalOrdering.java:29).

Call chain:
1. `Ordering.natural()` at Ordering.java:173-174 returns `NaturalOrdering.INSTANCE` — a singleton of type `NaturalOrdering` (not bare `Ordering<C>`).
2. `.reverse()` is then dispatched on the *runtime* type `NaturalOrdering`, which overrides `reverse()` at NaturalOrdering.java:70-71: `return (Ordering<S>) ReverseNaturalOrdering.INSTANCE;`
3. The concrete class returned is therefore `ReverseNaturalOrdering` — a separate singleton — NOT the generic wrapper `ReverseOrdering<NaturalOrdering>` that `Ordering.reverse()` at Ordering.java:415 would create.

Cross-check grep evidence:
- `grep -n "reverse()" guava/src/com/google/common/collect/NaturalOrdering.java` → line 70 confirms the override
- `grep -n "ReverseNaturalOrdering.INSTANCE" guava/src/com/google/common/collect/NaturalOrdering.java` → line 71 confirms the return value
- `grep -n "class ReverseNaturalOrdering" guava/src/com/google/common/collect/ReverseNaturalOrdering.java` → line 29 confirms the concrete class
result.omniweaveAnswer: omniweave callees "ImmutableSortedMap.reverseOrder" resolves "reverse" to Ordering.java:415 (the base-class method `new ReverseOrdering<>(this)`), NOT to NaturalOrdering.java:70 (the overriding method that returns `ReverseNaturalOrdering.INSTANCE`). omniweave uses static-type dispatch and therefore points to the wrong callee for this resolution step. However, a WITH-omniweave agent that further calls `omniweave node NaturalOrdering` or `omniweave callers ReverseNaturalOrdering` would discover that NaturalOrdering.reverse() is the actual dispatcher and that it returns ReverseNaturalOrdering.INSTANCE. omniweave callers of ReverseNaturalOrdering correctly shows: NaturalOrdering.java:68 → ReverseNaturalOrdering.
result.grepCanReach: partial — grep CAN reach the correct answer but requires a 3-hop manual trace: (1) read ImmutableSortedMap.java:618 to see `Ordering.<K>natural().reverse()`; (2) read Ordering.java:173-174 to discover `natural()` returns `NaturalOrdering.INSTANCE`; (3) specifically open NaturalOrdering.java to look for a `reverse()` override (the critical step a lazy agent would skip). A grep agent that stops at Ordering.java:415 (the base class `reverse()`) will conclude the return type is `ReverseOrdering` — which is wrong. Only by reading NaturalOrdering.java does the agent find the override returning `ReverseNaturalOrdering.INSTANCE`.
result.divergencePrediction: WITHOUT-arm agent will plausibly get this WRONG. The natural reading of `Ordering.<K>natural().reverse()` leads the agent to `Ordering.reverse()` at line 415, which returns `new ReverseOrdering<>(this)`. The agent would then report the concrete class as `ReverseOrdering`. The structural reason for failure: the static return type of `natural()` is `Ordering<C>`, so the dispatch for `.reverse()` is textually ambiguous — an agent without the type graph does not know (without reading NaturalOrdering.java) that NaturalOrdering overrides `reverse()` to return a completely different singleton class (`ReverseNaturalOrdering`) rather than the generic wrapper (`ReverseOrdering`). This is a concrete case where Java dynamic dispatch on a known-concrete-but-statically-typed variable requires reading the override in a separate file.
result.notes: The question is fair: a real developer tracing what Comparator an ImmutableSortedMap.reverseOrder() Builder uses would need to know which concrete Ordering is stored. The trap is plausible: Ordering has 14 subclasses, two of them (NaturalOrdering and ReverseNaturalOrdering) form a special symmetric pair with optimized singleton overrides of reverse(). The base-class Ordering.reverse() creates a generic ReverseOrdering wrapper, but NaturalOrdering short-circuits this to return the dedicated ReverseNaturalOrdering singleton. Both WITH and WITHOUT agents start from the same wrong callee (Ordering.java:415), but WITH-arm can recover via `omniweave callers ReverseNaturalOrdering` which shows NaturalOrdering as the only real caller.
